title: Characterizing Fill Factor Limitations in Perovskite-Silicon Tandem Solar Cells
authors: Yueming Wang, Nan Sun, Chris Dreessen, Gaosheng Huang, Alexander Eberst, Kaining Ding, Thomas Kirchartz
abstract: Perovskite-silicon tandem technology has exceeded the single junction theoretical efficiency limit. However, there is still distance to the thermodynamic limit mainly caused by the fill factor. This work presents a methodology to illustrate the mechanisms of FF loss in perovskite-Si monolithic tandem solar cells. Apart from the series resistance related loss characterized by electroluminescence, another loss factor is from the photoshunt, a phenomenon in which the parallel resistance apparently reduces under illumination in perovskite solar cells due to the moderate charge transport layer mobility. In addoition, the two-diode property of the Si cell can also influence the FF of tandem devices. The photoshunt can be hidden when the bottom cell is over illuminated, which explains highly efficient tandem solar cells are usually bottom cell limited. This work outlines strategies that overcoming the photoshunt issue can move the perovskite top cell closer to low FF losses in tandem solar cells.
summary: This paper investigates why perovskite-silicon tandem solar cells fail to reach their theoretical efficiency limits, focusing on the Fill Factor. The researchers identify a phenomenon called 'photoshunt,' where low mobility in the charge transport layers causes an apparent drop in parallel resistance under light. They demonstrate that this loss can be minimized by designing cells that are slightly limited by the bottom silicon cell's current. This work provides a roadmap for improving the efficiency of next-generation tandem photovoltaics.
main_problem: Identifying and characterizing the mechanisms responsible for Fill Factor (FF) losses in perovskite-silicon monolithic tandem solar cells, specifically distinguishing between series resistance and the 'photoshunt' effect.
main_result: The study identifies 'photoshunt'—an illumination-dependent reduction in parallel resistance due to low charge transport layer mobility—as a critical FF loss mechanism, and shows that operating in a 'bottom-cell limited' regime can mitigate this effect.
method: The authors combine experimental characterization (electroluminescence spectroscopy, EQE, and J-V measurements) with equivalent circuit modeling and analytical derivations to quantify resistive losses.
model_or_system: A monolithic two-terminal perovskite-silicon tandem solar cell consisting of a perovskite top cell (approx. 1.7 eV) and a silicon heterojunction bottom cell.
key_observables: Fill Factor (FF), short-circuit current density (Jsc), open-circuit voltage (Voc), series resistance (Rs), and photoshunt resistance (Rp,photo).
important_parameters: Charge transport layer mobility (mu_TL), bandgap (Eg), current matching conditions, and the radiative limit (Shockley-Queisser limit).
main_assumptions: The Shockley-Queisser model assumes radiative recombination is the only recombination mechanism and assumes a standard AM1.5G solar spectrum.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows efficiency losses relative to the SQ limit; Figure 2 shows SQ limits for varying bandgaps; Figure 3 shows EL spectra and reconstructed J-V curves; Figure 4 shows tandem efficiency and FF as functions of top-cell bandgap and photoshunt resistance; Figure 5 shows simulated J-V curves.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the efficiency gap relative to the SQ limit, introduces the experimental characterization via EL, presents the discovery of the photoshunt mechanism through analytical modeling, and concludes with design strategies for high-efficiency tandem cells.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. The relative losses of photovoltaic parameters of a selected group of high-efficiency single- junction and tandem solar cells are presented, normalized to their respective values within the framework of the SQ model. As the product of each ratio gives the normalized efficiency according to equation (1), and because multiplicative factors preserve their area on a logarithmic scale, the y-axis is a logarithmic axis in % of losses, whereby the lowest edge of each column represents the normalized efficiency 𝜂real 𝜂SQ ⁄ .
Figure 1. The blue sections of the bars in Figure 1 represent the loss due to imperfect photocurrent

Figure 2. Contour plots for the photovoltaic parameters in the radiative limit for (a) efficiencies, (b) short-circuit current densities, (c) open-circuit voltages, and (d) fill factors of monolithic 2-T tandem solar cells. All data are plotted as a function of the two band gaps of the two subcells.

Figure 3. (a) Injection-dependent absolute electroluminescence (EL) spectra of the perovskite and silicon sub-cells. Measurements were performed under various injected current to vary bias voltages. (b) reconstructed J-V curves calculated from injection-dependent electroluminescence measurements (sphere) The J-V measurement under 1-sun illumination of tandem cell is plotted in green. (c) the percentage of Shockley-Queisser efficiencies for perovskite-Si tandem solar cell and sub-cells in losses of current densities, voltages and fill factors corresponding to a multiplicative factor in equation (8) plotted on a logarithmic scale.

Figure 4. (a) Efficiency and (b) FF vs. bandgap for tandem solar cells with record heterojunction c-Si fixed as the bottom cell and perovskite top cells varying parallel resistance. (c) Efficiency and (d) FF vs. bandgap for different values of the parallel resistance of the single-junction perovskite solar cells.

Figure 5. Simulated J-V curve under 1 sun illumination of (a) perovskite-Si tandem solar cells and (b) single-junction perovskite solar cells. J-V curve shifted to the first quadrant by adding Jsc with varied parallel resistances 𝑅p,photo (from 102 to 106 Ωcm2). The bandgap of perovskite is 1.74 eV.

Figure 6. Calculated parallel resistance (Rp,photo ) (defined as 𝑅p,photo = 𝑑𝑉𝑑𝐽 ⁄ |𝑉=0) under illumination at one sun as a function of the mobility of charge transport layer using equation (13). Higher mobilities generally lead to higher shunt resistances. The exception are extremely low mobilities, where the series resistance becomes so high that the whole current-voltage curve becomes purely ohmic. In this case, the increasing series resistance with lower mobilities also drives up the measured parallel resistance.
